attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure consists of two panels. Panel (a) shows a 2D side-view line graph of linear interpolation. Panel (b) shows a 3D orthographic view of bilinear interpolation with the grid base aligned such that 'n,m' is at the front-left and 'n+1,m' is at the front-right, viewed from a slightly elevated angle looking down-left.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot swaps the panels, placing (b) on the left and (a) on the right. The view in the 3D panel is mirrored, showing 'n+1,m' on the front-left and 'n,m' on the front-right. Similarly, the 1D panel has 'n+1' on the left and 'n' on the right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The rendered view has both panels horizontally flipped/mirrored and swapped in layout compared to the source figure. This causes the slopes and coordinate layouts to run in opposite directions.

shape_comparison.source_shape: solid green line / transparent green surface
shape_comparison.rendered_shape: green cylinder lines / transparent green mesh

discrepancies: The layout is swapped: panel (a) is on the right and panel (b) is on the left, whereas the source has (a) on the left and (b) on the right.
The horizontal direction is completely mirrored in both panels, causing the values/labels of n and n+1 to be reversed.
The vertical projection line from (a,b) up to the surface is a dashed red line in the render, but a solid red line in the source.
The label '(b) Bilinear interpolation' severely overlaps with the 'n+1,m' label.
evaluation.notes: The figure's layout is completely mirrored and swapped left-to-right compared to the source, and there are no actual interactive controls besides passive orbit/hovering.
action_items: Swap the positions of Panel (a) and Panel (b) in the 3D scene to match the source layout.
Invert the X-coordinates of the geometry in both panels to correct the mirroring (so 'n' is on the left and 'n+1' is on the right).
Change the vertical dashed red line from (a,b) to a solid red cylinder line to match the source figure.
Adjust the vertical position of the panel labels to avoid overlapping with coordinate text.
Add interactive sliders or 2D control handles to let users dynamically move point 'a' and point '(a,b)' and see the interpolation update in real-time.

feedback.rationale: The rendering is horizontally flipped and swapped compared to the source, has overlapping labels, and completely lacks the necessary interactive controls, all of which are addressable in the next refinement step.

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows two panels: (a) on the left has a 2D-like front-facing view of a linear interpolation line rising from left to right; (b) on the right has a 3D oblique viewpoint looking down from the front-right-above, with the corner (n, m) closest to the viewer at the bottom-left, (n+1, m) to the bottom-right, and depth extending upwards-right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the two panels in reverse horizontal order: (b) Bilinear interpolation is on the left, and (a) Linear interpolation is on the right. The 3D bilinear panel is viewed from an azimuth that places the (n,m) corner on the bottom-right instead of the bottom-left.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The panel layout is reversed (left-right swap), and the 3D camera azimuth is mirrored/rotated, causing the corner labels and layout structure of the bilinear grid to be misaligned with the source figure.
discrepancies: The panels are swapped: (a) Linear interpolation is on the right instead of the left, and (b) Bilinear interpolation is on the left instead of the right.
The slope of the linear interpolation panel is reversed: in the source, it rises from left to right, whereas in the render, it falls from left to right.
The corner (n,m) in the bilinear panel is on the bottom-right instead of the bottom-left due to incorrect camera azimuth.
The label 'n,m\pm1' on the base of the bilinear plot is garbled/incorrectly combined compared to the clean individual corner labels in the source.
evaluation.notes: The mathematical implementation of the bilinear interpolation is highly interactive and correct, but the visual layout is completely reversed and the camera angle is wrong.
action_items: Swap the positions of the Linear and Bilinear panels (Linear on the left, Bilinear on the right).
Adjust the camera viewpoint (azimuth) so the 3D grid aligns correctly with the source, putting (n, m) at the front-left.
Invert the heights of L0 and L1 in the linear panel so the slope rises from left to right.
Clean up and separate base coordinate labels (e.g. ensure 'n, m+1' and 'n+1, m+1' are positioned correctly without overlapping or merging into 'n,m\pm1').

feedback.rationale: The layout has reversed panel positions, inverted slopes, wrong camera angles, and garbled corner labels, all of which are highly addressable visual and coordinate-system adjustments for the generator.
action_items: Swap the positions of the Linear and Bilinear panels (Linear on the left, Bilinear on the right).
Adjust the camera viewpoint (azimuth) so the 3D grid aligns correctly with the source, putting (n, m) at the front-left.
Invert the heights of L0 and L1 in the linear panel so the slope rises from left to right.
Clean up and separate base coordinate labels (e.g. ensure 'n, m+1' and 'n+1, m+1' are positioned correctly without overlapping or merging into 'n,m\pm1').

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a 3D isometric projection of bilinear interpolation. The base plane is viewed from a moderately high elevation, showing a clear, open quadrilateral base with depth. The camera is positioned looking from the front-right-above towards the back-left-down.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot views the 3D scene from a very low elevation angle. The base plane is squashed almost flat into a single horizontal line, causing depth to be severely compressed and elements along the depth axis to overlap.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The rendered view has a much lower elevation angle (is too flat) compared to the source figure. This extreme foreshortening causes the bottom-left labels and nodes to pile up and overlap heavily.
discrepancies: Severe label overlapping on the bottom-left corner of the bilinear panel where 'n,m' and 'n,m+1' are rendered directly on top of each other.
The camera angle is too low, causing the 3D base rectangle to appear nearly flat/squashed.
The green interpolation edges and black/dashed stems are excessively thick compared to the clean, thin lines of the original textbook illustration.
The left panel (Linear interpolation) is pushed too far to the left margin, clipping some visual elements.
evaluation.notes: The 3D visualization is technically correct but severely hampered by a flat camera viewpoint, thick primitives, and overlapping labels.
action_items: Increase the camera elevation angle (elevationDeg) so the base plane is viewed from further above, resolving the foreshortening and separating the overlapping labels.
Significantly reduce the radius of all cylinders representing lines and dashed segments to match the thin line style of the original diagram.
Add interactive HTML range sliders for parameters 'a' and 'b' to allow users to dynamically change the interpolation position in real-time.
Adjust the HTML label positions and offsets to eliminate text overlaps, particularly at the corner nodes.

feedback.rationale: The rendering has a squashed camera angle, excessively thick line primitives, overlapping labels, and lacks the required interactive sliders, all of which are highly addressable in a refinement step.
action_items: Increase the camera elevation angle (elevationDeg) so the base plane is viewed from further above, resolving the foreshortening and separating the overlapping labels.
Significantly reduce the radius of all cylinders representing lines and dashed segments to match the thin line style of the original diagram.
Add interactive HTML range sliders for parameters 'a' and 'b' to allow users to dynamically change the interpolation position in real-time.
Adjust the HTML label positions and offsets to eliminate text overlaps, particularly at the corner nodes.

gpt-5.5.failure_modes_analysis: The generated scene recreates the main interpolation structures with black/red nodes, green interpolation lines, dashed projection guides, and a translucent bilinear surface. However, the rendered view is badly framed: the left linear-interpolation panel is partially offscreen, its left endpoint and labels are clipped, the bottom title is cut off, and several right-panel labels/geometry are truncated at the canvas edges. The bilinear panel dominates the frame compared with the source, while the two-panel layout in the reference is much wider and fully visible.
gpt-5.5.failure_modes_reasoning: Missing-Labels applies because important annotations and captions are absent or clipped in the rendered view, including parts of the linear panel labels/title and some right-side labels. Camera-Wrong applies because the initial view/framing does not match the source: the content is cropped and shifted rather than showing the full two-panel textbook layout. Scale-Wrong applies because the right bilinear diagram is oversized relative to the left panel and the overall proportions do not match the wide balanced reference figure.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_analysis: The scene contains two separate constructions: a planar linear-interpolation panel with base axis, stems, input/output ellipsoid nodes, green interpolation segment, dashed projection, and labels; and a true 3D bilinear-interpolation panel with a rectangular base in XZ, four sample stems, corner samples, green interpolation edges/lines, dashed projection guides, red output/base nodes, labels, and a translucent bilinear surface. The bilinear portion is real 3D geometry rather than a flat pasted or skewed billboard, though one expected interpolated row marker such as the upper l_out(a,m+1) node/base guide point appears absent or underrepresented, and some node/guide placements are stylized rather than exact.
gpt-5.5.geometry_accuracy_reasoning: All major geometric components are present and constructed as genuine 3D primitives with plausible proportions and connections, with only minor omissions or alignment/styling inaccuracies preventing a perfect score.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_analysis: The code includes OrbitControls for rotate/zoom (functional but not counted), a hidden Reset View button with a click handler (not visible, so not usable), hover tooltips via pointermove/raycasting over registered objects, click handlers on registered scene objects that post explanatory popups, pointerleave/Escape handlers to hide tooltip/popup. Three scene targets are registered: the linear interpolation group, the bilinear surface mesh, and the bilinear group/final-value region; these should visibly show hover tooltips and trigger explanatory text when clicked, though the popup depends on the parent frame handling the postMessage. There are no sliders, toggles, parameter controls, or guided step-through animation; UI chrome is minimal and does not cover geometry.
gpt-5.5.interactivity_usability_reasoning: There are at least two functional pedagogical hover/click explanation interactions with compact UI, but no guided demo, so this fits score 4 rather than 5.
gpt-5.5.faithfulness_analysis: The rendered scene uses similar green lines/plane, black/red points, and math labels, so the interpolation concept is recognizable. However the default frame has a very different aspect ratio and crop from the source: the wide textbook figure is squeezed into a much narrower view, with the left linear panel cut off at the left and bottom caption cropped, while the right bilinear panel is over-zoomed and clipped at the top/right with several labels truncated or misplaced relative to the source.
gpt-5.5.faithfulness_reasoning: Although the main idea is present, the severe aspect-ratio, zoom, and cropping mismatches make it a poor drop-in replacement, matching well under 65% of the original composition.
gpt-5.5.label_quality_analysis: Source labels include, in panel (a): ℓ_in[n], ℓ_out(a), ℓ_in[n+1], bottom labels n, a, n+1, and caption “(a) Linear interpolation”; in panel (b): ℓ_in[n,m], ℓ_in[n,m+1], ℓ_in[n+1,m], ℓ_in[n+1,m+1], ℓ_out(n,b), ℓ_out(a,m), ℓ_out(a,m+1), ℓ_out(a,b), point label I(a,b), bottom/grid labels n,m, n,m+1, n+1,m, n+1,m+1, and caption “(b) Bilinear interpolation”. The generated figure shows some correct labels such as ℓ_out(a), ℓ_in[n+1], a, n+1, several bilinear ℓ labels, n,m, n,m+1, (a,b), and the bilinear caption, with generally readable font size. However, multiple source labels are missing or cropped off, including ℓ_in[n], bottom n, the “(a)” caption prefix, I(a,b), and several right-side bilinear labels are truncated or absent; some labels overlap or sit very close to geometry and the image crop cuts off text at the edges. Overall the label set is only partially complete, with acceptable font size but many missing/cropped labels and imperfect placement.
gpt-5.5.label_quality_reasoning: Most of the full source label set is incomplete or clipped despite some readable correct labels, so this fits score 2 rather than 3.
gpt-5.5.concept_accuracy_analysis: The figure clearly illustrates 1D linear interpolation and 2D bilinear interpolation with labeled input samples, output samples, base coordinates, projection guides, and a bilinear patch. The hover/click explanations are figure-specific and correctly describe the red interpolated samples, weighted averaging, the four-corner bilinear surface, and the two-step interpolation process; the only minor omission is that exact weights/equations such as (1-a), a, (1-b), b are not stated explicitly.
gpt-5.5.concept_accuracy_reasoning: Main concepts and interactions are accurate and specific, with only a minor missing detail in the lack of explicit interpolation formulas/weights.
